explicit_text: I was born in the village of Muang Cha in Laos on June 15th, 1950. This place is where many significant events of my life happened, including the crash of an American plane nearby during the Secret War. My family lived there and helped the American pilot who survived the crash. Muang Cha holds many memories of struggle and resilience for me and my family.

explicit_text: Laos is my homeland where I was born and lived through the difficult times of the Secret War. It was in Laos that my family supported American soldiers and faced the dangers from the Communist Party of Laos. Many of my loved ones, including my grandfather, were lost during this conflict. Laos shaped my early life and the hardships I endured before eventually moving to the United States in 1993.

explicit_text: I am a survivor of the Secret War in Laos, which brought much suffering to my family and community. During this war, my family helped American soldiers, including an American pilot who was injured after his plane crashed near our village. The war caused the death of my grandfather and many others close to me. It was a time of fear, resistance, and survival that deeply affected my life and the lives of many in Laos.

explicit_text: I arrived in the United States in 1993 after fleeing the hardships of war in Laos and Thailand. The United States became my new home where I could share my story and preserve the memories of my past. It was here that I reflected on the sacrifices made during the Secret War and honored those who helped us, including American soldiers. Living in the U.S. gave me a chance to rebuild my life and remember my heritage.

explicit_text: My husband is Colonel Shong Leng Xiong, a brave man who fought alongside us during the Secret War. He was a key figure in our community and taught me how to use weapons to defend ourselves. Together, we faced many dangers, including encounters with enemy forces. He was not only my husband but also my closest ally and protector during those turbulent times.

explicit_text: During the Secret War, my family helped an American pilot whose plane crashed near our village in Muang Cha. He was injured and hid in a cavern for two days, afraid that the communists would kill him. I personally fed him and gave him medicine to heal his wounded leg, showing him that we meant no harm. This pilot became a symbol of the friendship between my family and the American soldiers during the war.

explicit_text: After the war, my family and I fled to Thailand in 1985 to escape the violence in Laos. We lived there for several years before finally coming to the United States in 1993. Thailand was a place of refuge but also hardship as we waited for a chance to start a new life. It marked an important chapter in my journey from war survivor to immigrant.

explicit_text: The Communist Party of Laos was the opposing force during the Secret War that threatened my family and community. They were responsible for attacks that led to the death of my grandfather and others. My family had to hide and fight against their advances to survive. Their presence shaped much of the fear and conflict we experienced in Laos.

explicit_text: I remember one Thanksgiving during the war when an American soldier brought us two turkeys to eat. It was a special moment of kindness amidst the hardship and violence we faced. That day, sharing the turkey felt like a connection to the United States and a reminder of the friendship between our people. It remains a cherished memory of generosity during difficult times.
